card.title: Why You See Lightning Before You Hear Thunder
card.teaser: Light moves a million times faster than sound. That gap between flash and boom is physics you can count on your fingers.
card.coverImagePrompt: A dramatic lightning bolt splitting a dark purple stormcloud over a rain-soaked prairie, high-speed photography, electric and cinematic

lessons.title: Flash. One. Two. Three. Boom.
lessons.body: Watch a thunderstorm long enough and you'll notice: the flash always comes first, the rumble follows. Sometimes the gap is a second; sometimes ten. That gap isn't mystery. It's a mini physics lesson playing out across the sky, and once you know the trick, you can measure how far away the storm is just by counting.

visual.imagePrompt: A silhouetted child at a window watching lightning flash over distant hills, warm lamplight inside, quiet wonder

lessons.title: Two speeds, one event
lessons.body: Lightning and thunder are the same event. When a bolt of lightning heats the air to roughly 30,000 degrees Celsius — five times hotter than the Sun's surface — the air explodes outward. That explosion sends out two signals: light and sound. But light travels at about 300,000 kilometers per second, while sound trudges along at 343 meters per second.

keyTerms.definition: About 300,000 km per second — essentially instantaneous at everyday distances
About 343 meters per second in air at sea level — roughly a million times slower than light

visual.imagePrompt: A split-screen showing a light wave arriving instantly and a slower sound wave rippling outward from a lightning strike, infographic style

lessons.title: Counting the storm
lessons.body: Because sound covers about 343 meters per second — or roughly one kilometer every three seconds — you can estimate a storm's distance with simple counting. See the flash, start counting. Hear the thunder, stop. Divide by three for kilometers, or by five for miles. A count of nine? The storm is about three kilometers away. A count of three? That's close enough to take shelter.

visual.imagePrompt: A stopwatch in the foreground with dark storm clouds looming behind, dramatic contrast lighting

lessons.title: The sky's mail delivery
lessons.body: Light arrives like a text message — instant. Sound arrives like a paper letter — in its own time. The gap between them is real, measurable, and has been the same since storms existed. Next time you see a flash, count. You're doing real physics, in real time, with nothing but your own heartbeat.
lessons.funFact: If lightning strikes so close that the flash and thunder arrive together, you're within about 100 meters — dangerously close. Get indoors.

quiz.question: Roughly how far does sound travel in 3 seconds?
quiz.explanation: Sound travels about 343 meters per second, so in 3 seconds it covers roughly one kilometer.

quiz.question: Why do you see lightning before hearing thunder?
options: Light travels much faster than sound
Thunder starts later than the flash
quiz.explanation: Both happen at the same time, but light arrives almost instantly while sound takes several seconds per kilometer.

quiz.question: If you count 15 seconds between flash and thunder, roughly how far is the storm?
quiz.explanation: Dividing 15 by 3 gives about 5 kilometers — a simple way to estimate a storm's distance.

sources.title: NOAA National Severe Storms Laboratory: Lightning
